item.title: Malbodge
item.content:encoded:
For those of you who don't know, Malbodge is a programming language designed to be as hard to use as possible ;)

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Its rare to see a love story on this site, but they're usually between the oddest of couples and they never fail to be very sad.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Here with Yau30. I have to say, the first half is quite dry, and I can see why this has such a low rating. But I think the story ultimately absolves itself and makes this worth the read.

One minor note: why the whitespace after Description?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can see a lot of work went into this, but personally this feels like it's everything I don't particularly care for in modern SCPs. There's a point where a narrative has so much narrative that it really is better told in a Tale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's missing something. The narrative's nice and doesn't make the mistakes that many make like not mentioning the effects of later revelations earlier on and thus not breaking suspension of disbelief. But there's not much beyond that, stuff I'd like to see are things like more info on -A, how it's implanted "in the wild", how it was developed, if it's been reverse engineered to a degree and the effects of that and if it hasn't why, more information about the psychology and mind of 3483 as even if it's move to a more complex mind was inspired by a human mind it seems unlikely that it was or would be as human as it seems, for exsample as it is it seems like a noncentralised intelegence, what are the consequences of that? More detail for the old containment protocols, for exsample plans for if 3483 became actively hostile or how to prevent interference in anything critical, what has been learned from the code, what precidents have been set by the whole situation etc.

item.title: Re: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded:
This image is borrowed from:

Original Source: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:DTI-sagittal-fibers.jpg
Creator: Thomas Schultz
License: CC BY-SA 3.0

item.title: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded: Images used in posted articles must comply with the site's image policy. A source must be provided on the discussion page and the source must be compatible with the site's license. The author will be notified after this posting via Wikidot PM and will have 48 hours to properly source the image before it is edited out of the page.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really don't know why, but for what it's worth, this was the closest I've gotten to crying at an article. I feel that, despite any execution errors (of which I didn't spot), that makes this deserving of an upvote. So, +1. (I had +1'd a while ago, but realized I hadn't commented)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hShY6xZWVGE

I am digging everything you are trying to do here. I especially loved the trial bit - bureaucratic tyranny ftw!!!!

However, if I were to characterize the article as a whole, I'd call it half-baked. Roger and Aria's relationship kind of just happens - I didn't really care abot them mostly because I didn't feel like I had witnessed anything that gave them a bond. In addition, I don't buy an experienced Foundation operative killing her husband just because she was drunk and he cheated on her with his waifu, and much of the dialogue just doesn't read as very human/compy - especially the first interview and Aria's last couple messages. For the next iteration, if this doesn't work or you'd like to revise it, I'd advise a more thorogh proofread, too - and maybe to expand on Roger and Aria's interactions, while trimming some of the less personal segments, like the part where Dark Doctor #297 casts aspersions on the Aria's willingness to comply. Maybe their day at the beach! That was a nice image.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Some authors say that contemporary SCP articles should have a narrative. This article is an effort to make a narrative.

However, the SCP feels very generic as an anomaly. If there were anything that would make it distinct from all the other AIs that are SCPs, it is not as salient as it should be. With a relatively poor start, the rest of the article feels like a slog.

Perhaps the idea here functions better without the constraints of the SCP format?

item.title: Back online...
item.content:encoded: Thanks to Darkstuff, Fieldstone and ihp for their detailed feedback, and to everyone who convinced me this draft was worth not deleting.
